long_candidates.reason: 4 politicians buying MSFT: Warner, Boozman, Fetterman, Mcguire
3 politicians buying JPM: Fetterman, Keating, Khanna

decisions.reasoning: Strongest long signal in the set. 4 politicians across both chambers (Warner, Boozman, Fetterman, McGuire) bought within a ~6-week window — a genuine cluster. Warner sits on Senate Intelligence and Commerce committees with direct oversight of big tech/AI. Boozman on Appropriations. Positive news sentiment (50 articles, score 0.104) aligns with the trade direction rather than contradicting it. Trade amounts are modest, but cross-chamber consensus adds credibility. No offsetting sellers.
decisions.key_risk: Cluster may partly reflect public AI/cloud enthusiasm rather than non-public information. Modest trade sizes ($1K–$15K) limit insider conviction signal. MSFT already richly valued; any macro or antitrust shock could reverse quickly.

decisions.reasoning: 3-politician cluster buying JPM across a ~1-week window (May 13–19), including Ro Khanna at a larger $15K–$50K size. Cross-chamber (Senate + House). Banking sector has been resilient and politicians on financial oversight committees buying a top-tier bank is a mild positive signal. No sellers offsetting the move.
decisions.key_risk: Zero news sentiment score and no supporting articles — this trade is flying blind on public narrative. None of the three buyers are confirmed members of Senate Banking or House Financial Services committees, weakening the oversight-conflict signal. Could simply be index-like buying.

decisions.reasoning: Only 2 politicians buying (Boozman and Fetterman), which meets the minimum cluster threshold but is below the 3+ conviction bar. Both also bought MSFT, suggesting broad big-tech allocation rather than GOOGL-specific insight. No committee-specific conflict of interest identified for GOOGL. Signal is too thin to open a new long with conviction.
decisions.key_risk: Ongoing DOJ antitrust proceedings against Google represent headline risk that could swamp any mild political buy signal.

decisions.reasoning: Constellation Energy (nuclear/power) bought by both Boozman and Warner — two senators — within a 1-week window. Warner sits on Senate Intelligence and has broader energy exposure; Boozman on Appropriations with energy subcommittee relevance. Nuclear power is a direct beneficiary of AI data center energy demand and potential federal policy tailwinds, making this a plausible insider-informed trade rather than purely public-news-driven. 2-actor cluster is the minimum, but the sector-policy alignment elevates conviction slightly.
decisions.key_risk: Only 2 actors — below preferred 3+ threshold. Nuclear policy timelines are long and uncertain. Trade sizes are small ($1K–$15K), limiting confidence in the signal's strength.

decisions.reasoning: Warner and McGuire buying AAPL — only 2 actors with small amounts. Both also bought MSFT, suggesting broad tech index-style allocation rather than AAPL-specific conviction. No identifiable committee oversight conflict for AAPL from these two members. Signal is too weak and too generic to justify a new long entry.
decisions.key_risk: AAPL faces ongoing China revenue risk and tariff exposure; a weak political buy signal does not offset macro headwinds.

decisions.reasoning: Only 2 politicians selling (Fetterman and Gottheimer), and critically, Fetterman's sale was on April 3 — over 6 weeks before Gottheimer's May 20 sale. This is NOT a tight cluster; it is two isolated sells spread across nearly 7 weeks. Gottheimer (House) has no confirmed telecom committee oversight role. Short signals need genuine conviction — 2 politicians with a wide time spread, small amounts, and no committee conflict do not clear the bar. AT&T also has a relatively high dividend yield that creates a carry cost for shorts.
decisions.key_risk: Shorting a high-dividend telecom with weak signal and no committee oversight confirmation risks bleed from dividend carry and a potential sector rotation into defensives.

decisions.reasoning: 2 House members selling AMT (Evans and McCormick) within a ~2-day window is the tightest cluster on the sell side, but the signal remains very weak. Neither Evans nor McCormick sits on committees with direct REIT or telecom infrastructure oversight. AMT is a cell tower REIT — a niche asset class whose performance is driven by interest rates and carrier capex, not congressional action. Without committee oversight conflict, this reads as portfolio rebalancing. Shorts on REITs in a rate-sensitive environment carry significant reversal risk if rates fall.
decisions.key_risk: No committee oversight linkage, only 2 actors, small amounts. Any Fed rate cut signal could rapidly reprice REITs upward, creating painful short squeeze conditions.
